name: Lachesis
description: Lachesis, in Greek mythology, is one of the three Moirai, or Fates, who determine the course of human life. Her sisters are Clotho, who spins the thread of life, and Atropos, who cuts it. Lachesis is specifically responsible for measuring the thread of life, thus determining its length and, consequently, the duration of an individual's existence. Unlike her sisters who initiate and terminate life, Lachesis is the measurer, the allotter of destiny. She is often depicted with a measuring rod or a scroll, symbolizing her role in deciding the span of each mortal's life. Her decisions are considered immutable and unchangeable, representing the deterministic nature of fate in Greek belief. While the gods themselves are subject to fate, Lachesis and her sisters embody the very power that governs even the immortals. Her influence extends to the cosmic order, ensuring the balance between life and death.
subtitle: Allotter of Destiny

keyMyths.title: The Choice of Souls
keyMyths.description: In Plato's Republic, the Myth of Er describes a vision of the afterlife where souls choose their next lives. Lachesis, along with her sisters, is present, overseeing the selection process. Souls are presented with various patterns of life, each representing different virtues, vices, and circumstances. The souls then choose their destinies, which Lachesis confirms, thus setting the course for their next incarnation. This myth highlights the role of Lachesis in the cyclical nature of existence and the consequences of choices made in the afterlife.

keyMyths.title: The Power of the Fates
keyMyths.description: The Fates, including Lachesis, are frequently mentioned in various Greek tragedies and epics, showcasing their supreme authority over even the gods. While the gods can influence events and intervene in mortal affairs, they cannot ultimately alter the decrees of the Fates. This limitation underscores the pervasive belief in determinism within Greek culture. Characters often grapple with the knowledge of their predetermined fate, highlighting the tension between free will and destiny, a central theme in Greek literature. The inevitability of Lachesis's measurements emphasizes the limits of divine power.

keyMyths.title: Consulting the Oracle
keyMyths.description: While individuals cannot directly alter their fate as determined by Lachesis, they often sought guidance from oracles like Delphi in an attempt to understand their predetermined path. The oracle could provide insights into the challenges and opportunities that lay ahead, allowing individuals to prepare for their destiny. However, even with this knowledge, the underlying fate as measured by Lachesis remained fixed. This practice reflects a complex relationship between acceptance of fate and proactive engagement with life's circumstances. Seeking the oracle was a way to navigate the predetermined course with as much awareness as possible.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides an early account of the Moirai, including Lachesis, as daughters of Night.
